notes: The engineering layer preserves the verse's three claims: (1) the wearer's identity is preserved across body-changes (engineer: customer-need persists across rewrites); (2) loss of one body is no more grievous than loss of one garment (engineer: senior eventually relinquishes mourning over discarded codebases); (3) the verse functions as a corrective to misplaced grief (engineer: the grief is misplaced when the requirement walks intact). Crucially, the engineering layer does NOT distort the verse into 'embrace change' or 'all rewrites are good' — both falsifiability and counter-example explicitly carve out the case where rewrites destroy genuine work. The reverse-translate test (analog → verse) yields a stance compatible with the imperishable doctrine: the dehī-equivalent (customer-need) survives and the loss-grief is misplaced. PASS.
